text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC -- URGENT FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Margaret Chen
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Attending Oncologist: [on file]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret is a 63-year-old woman with stage IIIB (T3N3M0, now upstaged to stage IV M1c) EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma who presents urgently for discussion of new imaging findings showing disease progression after 14 months of first-line osi therapy, including newly identified brain metastases on MRI.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Margaret was originally diagnosed at age 62 with stage IIIB left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, poorly differentiated, with a primary mass measuring 4.2 cm at presentation and associated mediastinal (stations 4L, 5, 6) and ipsilateral supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Her tumor harbored an EGFR L858R exon 21 activating mutation (allele frequency 38%), with co-occurring TP53 R175H, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and NKX2-1 amplification on comprehensive NGS panel; PD-L1 TPS was 15%, and she was ALK- and ROS1-negative. After multidisciplinary tumor board review, she was started on Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as definitive first-line systemic therapy given her sensitizing EGFR mutation, with surgical resection deemed not feasible given N3 nodal disease.

Her initial response to Tagrisso was excellent. A three-month restaging CT showed a 50% reduction in the primary tumor (from 4.2 cm to 2.1 cm), with near-resolution of the supraclavicular adenopathy. At nine months, the primary mass had further decreased to 1.5 cm with minimal residual mediastinal adenopathy, and a surveillance brain MRI at that time was clear. She was tolerating therapy well through that period -- she developed a grade 2 acneiform rash early in the course, managed with topical clindamycin and oral doxycycline, which gradually improved to grade 1 by the nine-month visit. A grade 1 paronychia of the left index finger was noted at the nine-month check and was managed conservatively with warm soaks and topical antibiotics.

Her most recent scheduled restaging CT (fourteen months after initiation of Tagrisso) has now returned with concerning findings. The left upper lobe residual mass has grown from 1.5 cm to 2.8 cm -- so back up from where it had shrunk to, and now approaching the size it was at initial diagnosis. There is also a new left hilar lymph node that was not present on prior imaging, and a new 1.1 cm nodule in the right adrenal gland that was not identified on any previous study. Given these findings, an urgent brain MRI was obtained, which revealed three new enhancing lesions: a 1.4 cm lesion in the right frontal lobe, a 0.8 cm lesion in the right parietal region, and a 0.6 cm lesion in the left cerebellar hemisphere. None of these were present on the nine-month surveillance brain MRI.

Today she presents for urgent evaluation and management planning. She reports she has been feeling relatively well at home. She denies any new headaches, visual changes, nausea, vomiting, or balance difficulties. She has not noticed any weakness, numbness, or difficulty with speech or word finding. She reports mild fatigue that she has attributed to her underlying disease and treatment. She has not had fevers, chills, or significant weight loss recently. She does note some mild intermittent dyspnea on exertion that has been slowly creeping back over the past six to eight weeks -- she had been doing quite well from a pulmonary standpoint during her period of response.

She has been adherent to Tagrisso throughout the course of therapy, taking it consistently with food as instructed. She has not missed doses and denies any recent illnesses that would have affected absorption.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Mild fatigue (baseline), mild dyspnea on moderate exertion (new over past 6-8 weeks, increased from prior), occasional dry cough.
Negative: No headache, no focal neurological symptoms, no visual disturbance, no ataxia or gait instability, no seizure activity, no syncope. No nausea or vomiting. No chest pain or pleuritic symptoms. No new bone pain. No abdominal pain or change in bowel habits. No hematuria. No weight loss in past 2 months. Skin rash improved and stable. No significant diarrhea currently (on loperamide PRN, using rarely).

---

PAST ONCOLOGIC HISTORY
As above; stage IIIB (now IV) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
- Hypertension, well-controlled on lisinopril
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin
- Former smoker, 25 pack-year history, quit approximately 8-9 years ago
- No prior history of autoimmune disease, interstitial lung disease

SURGICAL HISTORY
- No prior thoracic procedures aside from CT-guided biopsy at diagnosis

FAMILY HISTORY
- Not previously documented as pertinent to oncologic workup at this visit; no known family history of lung malignancy documented.

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired accountant. Lives with husband. Non-drinker. Former smoker (25 pack-years, quit ~8-9 years ago). Good social support at home.

ALLERGIES
NKDA (no known drug allergies)

MEDICATIONS
1. Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily [CURRENTLY HELD pending further evaluation]
2. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
3. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes)
4. Doxycycline 100 mg orally once daily (ongoing, for acneiform rash prophylaxis)
5. Loperamide 2 mg orally PRN diarrhea (using rarely)
6. Topical clindamycin solution applied to face/upper chest daily
7. Low-dose aspirin 81 mg orally daily (per her PCP)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Margaret appears well-developed, well-nourished, in no acute distress. She is alert and oriented x3. Conversational, cognitively intact. No acute distress.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 98.4 degrees F, weight 64.8 kg (stable from last visit).
Performance Status: ECOG 1.
HEENT: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact. Visual fields grossly full to confrontation. No papilledema appreciated on fundoscopic exam (limited exam in clinic). Facial skin with resolving acneiform eruption, mild residual erythema over malar regions and forehead, no pustules or nodules -- improved from prior visits. Oral mucosa moist, no ulcers or stomatitis. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable at this time (previously noted left supraclavicular node -- no longer palpable).
Respiratory: Breath sounds decreased at the left base; mild dullness to percussion at left base. No egophony. No wheeze. Right lung fields clear to auscultation throughout. No crackles.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly appreciated. No palpable right upper quadrant mass. Bowel sounds normal.
Neurological: Alert, oriented, speech fluent and appropriate. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Sensation intact to light touch in upper and lower extremities. Finger-nose-finger coordination intact bilaterally. Heel-shin testing intact. Gait steady, tandem gait intact. No pronator drift. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ symmetrically. Romberg negative.
Left index finger: Mild periungual erythema (paronychia, improved from prior, grade 1).
Skin: Mild residual acneiform rash on face and upper chest as noted, improved.

---

RELEVANT LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC (recent): WBC 7.2 x10^9/L, Hgb 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, stable), Plt 312 x10^9/L, ANC 4.1 x10^9/L.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138, Potassium 4.1, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60), BUN 14, AST 28, ALT 24, Alkaline phosphatase 88, Total bilirubin 0.6, Albumin 3.9. LDH 247 U/L (mildly elevated, up from 198 at last check).
Fasting glucose: 118 mg/dL (acceptable, on metformin).
HbA1c (last checked 3 months ago): 7.1%.
ECG: Sinus rhythm, rate 76. QTc 441 ms. No significant interval change from prior.

---

IMAGING REVIEWED
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (most recent, performed this month):
Compared to the prior CT at approximately 9 months of treatment, there is clear evidence of disease progression. The left upper lobe mass has grown from 1.5 cm back up to 2.8 cm -- it had previously shrunk down to a sub-2 cm residual lesion at best response. There is a new left hilar lymph node enlargement not previously appreciated. A new 1.1 cm nodule in the right adrenal gland is identified, suspicious for a new metastatic deposit. There are no new pulmonary nodules in the contralateral lung and no new obvious mediastinal adenopathy beyond the hilar finding. No obvious liver lesions on this CT. Small amount of fluid at the left base, though not a frank effusion.

MRI Brain with and without gadolinium (obtained urgently after CT findings):
Three new enhancing lesions are identified that were not present on the surveillance MRI nine months ago: a 1.4 cm lesion in the right frontal lobe, a 0.8 cm lesion in the right parietal region, and a 0.6 cm lesion in the left cerebellar hemisphere. All three demonstrate ring-like or nodular enhancement consistent with metastatic deposits. There is minimal surrounding edema and no significant mass effect. No leptomeningeal enhancement or dissemination identified. Given the size and number of lesions, she would be a candidate for stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) rather than whole-brain radiation, and she is neurologically intact at this time.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Margaret Chen is a 63-year-old woman with stage IV (upstaged from IIIB at diagnosis, now with adrenal and brain metastases) EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma who has documented systemic and intracranial progression after 14 months of first-line osi 80 mg daily. Her disease course has been notable for an excellent initial partial response -- the primary mass shrank from 4.2 cm down to 1.5 cm, and regional lymphadenopathy substantially resolved -- followed by classic acquired resistance at approximately 14 months, which is consistent with the known median progression-free survival data for Tagrisso in advanced EGFR-mutant NSCLC. She is now showing radiographic evidence of both extracranial (growing primary, new hilar node, new adrenal lesion) and intracranial (three new brain metastases) progression.

She is neurologically intact with an ECOG PS of 1 and remains ambulatory, active, and cognitively sharp. There is no headache, no seizure, no focal deficit, and no signs of elevated intracranial pressure on exam today. This is reassuring and affords us time to conduct appropriate workup before committing to a next systemic therapy.

Key management decisions addressed today:

1. osi -- HELD: osi has been discontinued today given documented progression. There is no role for continuing osi beyond progression in this setting without understanding the resistance mechanism, particularly because the treatment plan will depend heavily on what has driven resistance. Continuing Tagrisso empirically beyond progression in the context of brain metastases is not appropriate here.

2. RESISTANCE MECHANISM PROFILING -- LIQUID BIOPSY ORDERED: A cell-free DNA (cfDNA) plasma-based liquid biopsy has been ordered (Guardant360 or equivalent comprehensive circulating tumor DNA panel) to probe for acquired resistance mutations. The most common mechanism of acquired Tagrisso resistance in the context of the L858R driver mutation would be T790M in-trans acquisition (though T790M is already the basis of Tagrisso's superiority over prior-generation TKIs -- in third-gen TKI setting, C797S trans or cis mutations, MET amplification, KRAS mutations, histologic transformation to small cell carcinoma, and others have been reported). Understanding her specific resistance mechanism will be critical in selecting next therapy. Liquid biopsy results typically return within 7-10 business days.

3. RE-BIOPSY OF RIGHT ADRENAL NODULE: Given the new 1.1 cm right adrenal lesion, we have placed an urgent referral to interventional radiology for CT-guided right adrenal core needle biopsy. This serves two purposes: (a) histologic confirmation that this is indeed a metastatic deposit from her lung adenocarcinoma (and not an adrenal adenoma, which is common incidentally), and (b) tissue-based molecular profiling including comprehensive NGS and specific EGFR resistance mutation testing from this new lesion. We discussed the importance of having fresh tissue from a progressing lesion to best characterize resistance, as primary biopsy specimens are now over 14 months old and may not reflect the current molecular landscape.

4. STEREOTACTIC RADIOSURGERY (SRS) REFERRAL -- URGENT: Given three brain metastases, all measuring 1.6 cm or smaller, with no mass effect and a neurologically intact patient, she is an excellent candidate for stereotactic radiosurgery rather than whole-brain radiotherapy. Preserving neurocognitive function is an important quality-of-life priority, particularly given her excellent performance status and potential for meaningful disease control with appropriate subsequent systemic therapy. An urgent referral has been placed to our radiation oncology colleagues for Gamma Knife or frameless SRS planning. I have spoken to radiation oncology today and they anticipate being able to see her within the week for consultation and to proceed expeditiously with treatment planning. I emphasized the importance of treating the brain lesions while they remain small and asymptomatic.

5. SYSTEMIC THERAPY DECISION -- DEFERRED PENDING WORKUP: We will not commit to a specific next-line systemic regimen until we have the results of the liquid biopsy and the adrenal tissue biopsy/NGS. The decision for next therapy will take into account: (a) the specific resistance mutation identified, (b) any histologic transformation, (c) her overall performance status after SRS recovery, and (d) eligibility for clinical trials. I have already initiated a preliminary search of available clinical trial options at our institution and partner sites for EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-osi progression. We will discuss specific trial opportunities at our next visit once genomic data are available. Platinum-based chemotherapy (carbo + peme) is a standard second-line option for EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma progressing on an EGFR TKI, and we will certainly revisit that if no better-matched trial is available.

6. DEXAMETHASONE: Given the small size of the brain lesions, lack of edema, and current neurological intactness, I am not initiating prophylactic steroids at this time. If she develops neurological symptoms prior to SRS, she should call our nurse line immediately and we would initiate dexamethasone promptly. She and her husband have been counseled on specific symptoms to watch for (worsening headache, focal weakness, balance problems, new confusion) and instructed to seek emergency evaluation immediately if any of these develop.

7. SUPPORTIVE CARE: Continuing metformin for diabetes management. Lisinopril continued for hypertension. Topical clindamycin and doxycycline may be tapered given that the acneiform rash is now grade 1 and improving -- we will revisit this depending on what next systemic agent is chosen. Paronychia of left index finger is improving, continue warm soaks.

8. RETURN VISIT: I will see Margaret back in the clinic in 1-2 weeks, once liquid biopsy results return and after her radiation oncology consultation, to review all data and finalize next systemic therapy decision. I have asked her to call our nurse triage line immediately for any neurological changes in the interim.

The situation was discussed at length with Margaret and her husband. They understand that this represents a significant disease milestone -- a loss of response to her initial targeted therapy -- but I emphasized that we have additional treatment options, that she is in excellent clinical condition to proceed with further treatment, and that our goal remains to achieve meaningful and durable disease control. They are understandably anxious but remain engaged and motivated. They have questions about what comes next, which we will address more fully once we have the resistance mutation profile in hand.

---

Electronically signed: [Attending Oncologist, Thoracic Oncology]
Dictated and reviewed by: [Attending]
CC: Radiation Oncology, Interventional Radiology, Primary Care Physician
